message:
docs(readme): correct ft --version claim, add ft version --full (ft-b4mif)

README Quickstart (bcc668a0) claimed `ft --version` emits rustc + target
+ features, but the clap wiring at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:67-72
binds CLAP_VERSION = build_meta::short_version() which only returns
semver + short git commit (+dirty marker). The full block lives in
build_meta::verbose_version() and is reached only via `ft version --full`
(main.rs:16718-16724).

Fix is path (b) from the review bead: leave the binary behavior alone
(scripts grepping `ft --version` keep the short form) and correct the
README to show both commands — `ft --version` for the short line and
`ft version --full` for rustc/target/features/built. Matches what the
code actually does today.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -60,6 +60,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-7ojbj","title":"rate_limit_tracker: parse_retry_after can produce Duration that panics Instant + cooldown","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/rate_limit_tracker.rs:441-473` `parse_retry_after` has no upper bound on the parsed duration.

Plain-number path at :452-454:
```rust
if let Ok(secs) = text.parse::<u64>() {
    return Some(Duration::from_secs(secs));
}
```

'N unit' path at :458-469 has a `checked_mul` guard, but the plain number path returns `Duration::from_secs(u64::MAX)` untouched (u64::MAX secs ≈ 584 billion years).

The returned Duration flows into `PaneRateLimitState::record_event` at :164:
```rust
let expiry = event.detected_at + event.cooldown;
```

`Instant + Duration` panics when the sum overflows the platform's underlying representation. On macOS/Linux Instant is backed by clock_gettime timespec/uptime — adding ~584B years to a fresh Instant will overflow.

# Reachability

`retry_after_text` reaches record_event from pattern detection output. `trigger_retry_after` (workflows/builtin_workflows.rs) extracts the string from agent-emitted rate-limit messages. A malformed provider response or adversarial agent output ('Retry-After: 18446744073709551615') would panic the tracker on record.

# Existing tests

`parse_retry_after_overflow_returns_none` at :515-518 covers the 'N unit' checked_mul path with 'u64::MAX hours', but NOT the plain-number path with 'u64::MAX'. That path currently returns `Some` unclamped.

# Fix

Clamp parse_retry_after to a sane max (24h ceiling — legitimate provider Retry-After values never exceed 1h in practice, and any longer wait is a signal to bail rather than block the tracker). Apply the cap uniformly to plain-number and 'N unit' paths. Also clamp DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS fallback if it ever grows.

Add regression test: parse_retry_after_plain_u64_max_is_clamped asserts the plain-number path returns a Duration ≤ the cap, not None and not u64::MAX.

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires adversarial/malformed provider input to trigger. Tracker is behind rate_limit_tracker.lock() in workflows/builtin_workflows.rs so the panic takes down the workflow task but is scoped to one pane's rate-limit processing. Fix is trivial: one `.min(MAX_COOLDOWN)` call plus one test.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:08.211722Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213965Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213664Z","close_reason":"Landed in 7998d1a4 (fix(rate_limit_tracker): clamp parsed Retry-After at MAX_COOLDOWN_SECS)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-7quzz","title":"headless_mux_server: server_config_serde test missing max_peers after ft-ry224 added the field","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/headless_mux_server.rs:1116 has a ServerConfig struct literal in test server_config_serde that pre-dates ft-ry224's addition of max_peers: u32 (line 120). The test now fails to compile with E0063. Trivial: add max_peers: <value> to the literal.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:43:29.638577Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:51:45.829569Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:51:45.829266Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.
- Adversary-reachable via fixture/replay/fuzz inputs.
- Same arithmetic-poison family as ft-761tz (disk_pressure NaN) and
  ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure NaN): a hostile or pathological
  numeric input cascades into permanently-wrong tier/anomaly outputs
  with no self-recovery.

## Fix sketch

1. Replace \`prev + future_skew_threshold\` with \`prev.checked_add(threshold)\`.
2. On overflow, treat as "no future-skew anomaly" — we cannot prove a
   timestamp is too far in the future relative to a near-MAX baseline,
   so the only sound default is \"don't flag\". Same disposition as
   ft-761tz: when the math itself becomes unreliable, fail toward
   silence rather than toward false positives.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) overflow with threshold=1 and
   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` baseline-update behavior is
intentional and out of scope.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:32.110899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255991Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255609Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4431483f: detect_clock_anomaly now uses prev.checked_add(future_skew_threshold), failing silent on overflow rather than panicking (debug) or wrapping (release). 5 regression tests added covering (current=prev=u64::MAX, threshold=1), (huge threshold), (boundary u64::MAX × u64::MAX), (in-range path still fires), and (tracker survives MAX baseline). All 53 event_id tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-761tz (NaN) and ft-yskcu (NaN cascade).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-7ycug","title":"[review][LOW] ea7f89f9 GUI test coverage: tests assert type stability, not rendering correctness","description":"ft-1memj.28 commit ea7f89f9 adds 5 GUI unit tests across crates/frankenterm-gui/src/{glyphcache,quad,overlay/quickselect}.rs. Audit finding: tests pass superficial coverage bar but leave real rendering edges uncovered.

Specifics:

1. glyphcache.rs test_atlas_rect_lookup and atlas_rect_lookup_separates_distinct_cached_colors verify that cached_color memoizes and that distinct colors get distinct origins. That's a hash-map property test, not an atlas-rect-lookup test. Zero coverage for:
   - Atlas-full path (OutOfTextureSpace branch in glyphcache).
   - 2x HiDPI scale — cached_color takes only (color, alpha), no DPI param; glyph rasterization at 2x scale is untested.
   - Unicode wide-width / emoji / combining marks — glyphcache has separate code paths for these (see Underline + combining handling in the same file).
   - Atlas eviction under pressure.

2. overlay/quickselect.rs extracts two helpers (merge_dirty_results, clear_rendered_dirty_result) and tests them. clear_rendered_dirty_result is a one-line wrapper around RangeSet::remove with zero added logic — the test is effectively 'does RangeSet::remove work'. merge_dirty_results test is legitimate coverage of the intersect+clip behavior. But neither exercises:
   - sequence-number ordering of dirty merges during active search.
   - dirty-row propagation when search_row changes (the line 611/705 call sites interact with pattern-match highlighting).

3. quad.rs test_cell_vertex_generation overlaps with the existing quad_propagates_colors_* test two asserts below it. Both call set_position + set_texture_discrete and check vertex coordinates. Incremental coverage value is near zero.

Verdict: coverage-metric churn, not regression-catching tests. The ft-1memj.28 bead was 'add gui unit test coverage' so the commit technically closes it, but the tests would not have caught historical GUI bugs (atlas overflow, HiDPI scaling, wide-glyph clipping, dirty-row propagation during search).

Severity: LOW — not a bug, but a test-quality finding to flag for future test passes. Not recommending a fix commit here; file for visibility when the next ft-1memj.x GUI test pass lands.

 {"id":"ft-84ulk","title":"workflow runner releases pane lock on pre-start cx cancellation","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:07:27.230187Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:04.275236Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:04.274845Z","close_reason":"Released pane lock and marked execution failed when run_workflow_with_cx sees a pre-start cancelled child context; added regression coverage for the persisted-record + lock cleanup path.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-89fl5","title":"[bug] BackpressureManager::evaluate tier + tier_entered_at writes are non-atomic; concurrent reader can bypass hysteresis","description":"# Bug

backpressure.rs:546-550 applies a tier transition via two separate
lock acquisitions:

    *self.current_tier.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = proposed;
    *self
        .tier_entered_at
        .write()
        .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = Instant::now();

Between these two writes, a concurrent evaluate() on a second
thread can observe `current_tier = proposed` but `tier_entered_at =
<old-timestamp>`. That thread's hysteresis check at line 540:

    let entered = *self.tier_entered_at.read()...;
    let elapsed_ms = u64::try_from(entered.elapsed().as_millis())...;
    if elapsed_ms < self.config.hysteresis_ms {
        return None;
    }

uses the STALE `entered` value. Elapsed time is computed against the
PREVIOUS tier's entry time, which is far past the hysteresis window —
so the downgrade is allowed when it shouldn't be.

# Scenario

1. T=0: tier=Green, entered_at=T0.
2. T=100s: T1 evaluate() proposes Red (upgrade). Writes
   current_tier=Red. Scheduler pauses T1 before `tier_entered_at`
   write.
3. T=100.001s: T2 evaluate() reads current_tier=Red, proposes Green
   (downgrade). Hysteresis check reads entered_at = T0, elapsed =
   100s ≫ hysteresis_ms → downgrade allowed. Writes
   current_tier=Green.
4. T=100.002s: T1 resumes, writes tier_entered_at=100s.

Final state: current_tier=Green, tier_entered_at=100s. Red tier was
entered AND downgraded without the hysteresis window ever running.

# When does this matter

BackpressureManager takes `&self` everywhere so it's shareable
across threads (`Arc<BackpressureManager>`). Today there's only one
active evaluator task in the runtime (runtime.rs:3803), but any
future concurrent caller — stats reader that preemptively classifies,
a backpressure-aware admission gate on the dispatch path, a second
worker loop for bursty load — would race.

Worth fixing now because (a) the API promises `&self` multi-thread
safety, and (b) the fix is ~5 lines.

# Fix

Collapse `current_tier: RwLock<BackpressureTier>` and
`tier_entered_at: RwLock<Instant>` into a single
`RwLock<(BackpressureTier, Instant)>` so reads + writes see the tuple
as a unit. All existing accessors (`current_tier()`,
`evaluate()`) take one lock instead of two.

# Test

`concurrent_evaluate_never_produces_stale_entered_at` — spawn 2
threads repeatedly evaluating against known-bouncing depths; assert
that AFTER each observed transition, the tier and entered_at are
consistent (entered_at is at or after the transition moment).

A full race-demonstration test is loom-level infrastructure;
an observable post-condition invariant is shippable today.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:52:29.956478Z","created_by":"pane3-bp-atomic","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286781Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286495Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backpressure","concurrency","hysteresis"]}
 {"id":"ft-8ihuz","title":"session_restore::load_scrollback_data loads ALL segments up to max_seq before truncating, memory-blows on large scrollbacks","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:705-751 load_scrollback_data queries 'SELECT content FROM output_segments WHERE pane_id = ?1 AND seq <= ?2 ORDER BY seq ASC' with NO LIMIT. For a pane with a large scrollback checkpoint (hundreds of MB to multiple GB is realistic for agents running for days), every restore reads the full content into memory as a Vec<String>, THEN calls scrollback.truncate(max_lines) at line 746 which drops everything except the last N. Default restore_max_lines = 5000 (session_restore.rs:142), so for 1M segments totaling 1 GB, we allocate 1 GB and keep ~5 KB. The allocation spike can OOM frankenterm on startup of a large session. Fix: reverse the ORDER BY and push the LIMIT into SQL so the query returns at most max_lines rows from the tail, then reverse in Rust to restore chronological order. This bounds memory at max_lines × avg_segment_size (~ tens of MB worst case). Same truncation semantics (keep most-recent lines) preserved.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:04.442611Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:27:49.985139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:27:49.984775Z","close_reason":"Landed in 459fadc4 (fix(session_restore): bound load_scrollback_data by max_lines via SQL LIMIT). Flipped ORDER BY to DESC, added LIMIT ?3 with max_lines, reversed in Rust to restore chronological order. Regression test load_scrollback_data_respects_max_lines_limit_ft_8ihuz pins 5-of-50 clamp + chronological order. Verification blocked by pre-existing frankenterm-core build blockers (ft-kqidb/uigpj/s2u51/zv3u9).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
@@ -113,6 +114,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-azjol","title":"[sec] ipc.rs: non-constant-time token compare (HIGH)","description":"CVSS v3.1 AV:L/AC:H/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N ~5.7. Fixed in 5810a3a4. IpcAuth::authorize() used String::eq which short-circuits on first byte mismatch, plus .find() which short-circuits on first candidate match. Both are exploitable via timing side-channel over Unix socket: co-tenant process can recover the stored IPC token byte-by-byte AND distinguish token position in the vec. Fix: constant-time byte XOR accumulator in ipc_constant_time_eq + explicit loop in authorize() that visits every candidate. 7 regression tests. Found during /security-audit-for-saas adapted for CLI control-plane (pane6 phase C).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:27.060545Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:31.327952Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:31.327699Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 5810a3a4","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b4kpu","title":"Fix no-default-features lib blockers in search_explain and storage FTS helpers","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:23:16.262625Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:26:53.198935Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:26:53.198610Z","close_reason":"Stale failure snapshot: current main is internally consistent again after 1ad5c8cc landed the storage-side FTS rename that was racing the verifier.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b4l62","title":"[MEDIUM] telemetry Histogram NaN-poisons total_sum/mean and emits nonsense quantiles on NaN q","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure paths

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs`, `struct Histogram`.

### 1. `Histogram::record(NaN)` permanently poisons the running mean

```rust
pub fn record(&mut self, value: f64) {
    self.total_count += 1;
    self.total_sum += value;    // NaN poisons total_sum permanently
    if value < self.min { self.min = value; }
    if value > self.max { self.max = value; }
    ...
}
```

Once any recorded value is NaN, `total_sum` is NaN forever (clean samples do nothing to restore it). Then `mean()` returns `Some(NaN)`:

```rust
pub fn mean(&self) -> Option<f64> {
    if self.total_count == 0 { return None; }
    Some(self.total_sum / self.total_count as f64)
}
```

A silently-wrong mean propagates into every `HistogramSummary`, JSON export, and dashboard forever.

### 2. `Histogram::quantile(NaN)` returns an arbitrary sample

```rust
let idx = ((sorted.len() as f64 - 1.0) * q.clamp(0.0, 1.0)) as usize;
```

`f64::clamp(NaN, 0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN (documented gotcha). `(NaN) as usize` is 0 in Rust. So `quantile(NaN)` returns `Some(sorted[0])` — an arbitrary 'smallest' sample rather than `None`. Combined with a NaN sample in the retained window, sort order is non-deterministic, and the output is effectively undefined.

### 3. Classify: silent wrong-answer

Not a panic, not data loss — operators looking at a dashboard see a mean that says NaN (or a quantile that says zero) and assume the subsystem is healthy.

## Reachability

`Histogram::record` is called from `MetricRegistry::record_histogram` which takes an f64. Callers that pass `dt.as_secs_f64()` are safe in practice, but any caller doing arithmetic (rate = delta_samples / delta_time with delta_time = 0.0) can produce NaN. Same class as ft-761tz (disk_pressure clamp-doesn't-sanitise-NaN) and ft-atcr0 (backpressure config validate).

## Fix

1. `Histogram::record`: if `value.is_nan()`, return early (no counter bump, no sum update, no sample push). This matches ft-761tz's fail-closed precedent.
2. `Histogram::quantile`: if `q.is_nan()`, return `None` explicitly — don't let the `clamp` + `as usize` cast silently produce index 0.
3. Regression tests covering both defenses.

## Scope

Single file (`telemetry.rs`). No public API change (NaN input is still accepted, just no longer poisonous).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:39:42.897340Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:46:08.381499Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:46:08.380804Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-b4mif","title":"[review][MEDIUM] README Quickstart claims ft --version emits rustc/target/features but it only emits semver+git","description":"README Quickstart section (landed in bcc668a0, Apr 23 13:54) claims:

  ft --version                 # semver + git commit + rustc + target + features

The clap version wiring at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:67-72 binds version = CLAP_VERSION.as_str() where CLAP_VERSION = build_meta::short_version() (lines 39-41). short_version() returns only:

  format!("{} ({}{})", frankenterm_core::VERSION, GIT_HASH, GIT_DIRTY)

i.e. semver + git commit + optional dirty marker. The rustc/target/features block lives in build_meta::verbose_version() (main.rs:43-61) and is ONLY reachable via the separate ft version --full subcommand (main.rs:16718-16724), NOT ft --version.

Without #[command(long_version = ...)] in the clap derive, ft --version and ft -V both surface short_version() only. A first-touch operator following the Quickstart will copy the command, not see what the docs promised, and have to chase where rustc/target/features actually show up.

Fix options:
1. Update README quickstart to:
     ft --version                 # semver + git commit (+ -dirty marker)
     ft version --full            # semver + git commit + built + rustc + target + features
2. OR add long_version = build_meta::verbose_version() to the Cli derive at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:72 so ft --version actually emits the full block (ft -V stays short).

Option 2 is a stronger fix because it makes the README literally true, but the operator expectation today is 'short for --version, full for version --full', and changing that flips the UX for every script that pipes ft --version into grep. Option 1 (doc fix) is safer.

Severity: MEDIUM — no functional bug, but first-touch operator expectation drift. Discovered in reviewer pane audit round 2, 2026-04-23.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:01:52.738804Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:04:05.861489Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-b5rbd","title":"[HIGH] GUI resize increment saturation can overflow backend min-size arithmetic","description":"Commit 193a9ebb changes `ResizeIncrementCalculator` to saturate `base_width`/`base_height` at the u16 ceiling (`crates/frankenterm-gui/src/resize_increment_calculator.rs:15-35`). Multiple window backends then immediately compute `base + increment` in u16 without guarding overflow (`frankenterm/window/src/os/macos/window.rs:1410-1412`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/x11/window.rs:1892-1893`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/wayland/window.rs:915-916`). With large padding/border/tab-bar geometry, the new saturation can produce `base_width == u16::MAX`, so `base_width + x` overflows before min-size clamping. This turns an intended overflow hardening change into a runtime panic/wraparound path on oversized layouts. Expected fix: clamp stored base dimensions to `u16::MAX - increment` (or widen backend arithmetic) and add a regression test that asserts `base + increment` stays representable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:58:04.554054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945504Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945232Z","close_reason":"dup of ft-htf0i","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b677n","title":"[MEDIUM] wgpu readback poll loop has no test coverage for timeout, disconnect, or error-propagation paths","description":"Commit 6f41130c (br-43bbl fix wgpu readback polling) rewrites the
readback wait in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193
from:

\`\`\`rust
self.device.poll(PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) })?;
receiver.recv_timeout(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT)?...
\`\`\`

to a bounded poll loop:

\`\`\`rust
let deadline = Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT;
loop {
    self.device.poll(wgpu::PollType::Poll)?;
    match receiver.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(10)) {
        Ok(result) => { result?; break; }
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) if Instant::now() < deadline => continue,
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) => return Err(...),
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected) => return Err(...),
    }
}
\`\`\`

Concerns with the new implementation:

1. \`PollType::Poll\` is non-blocking by contract in wgpu 25. The loop
   relies on \`Poll\` actually driving the \`map_async\` callback forward.
   If a future wgpu point release changes \`Poll\` to be strictly
   \"dispatch-only\" (no callback invocation), the loop would always
   hit the 5s timeout. There is no test pinning this behavior.

2. The \`Disconnected\` branch is reachable only if the \`map_async\`
   closure drops without sending — that happens e.g., if wgpu drops
   the submission without invoking the callback (device lost, internal
   error). No test exercises this path.

3. \`Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT\` panics on overflow
   (impossible in practice for 5s, but worth noting as a latent
   concern). Prefer \`checked_add\`.

4. Busy-wait cost: on the timeout path, the loop spins ~500 iterations
   (5000ms / 10ms) calling \`device.poll\` each time. The prior
   \`PollType::Wait\` blocked in the driver. This is a CPU-efficiency
   regression on cancellation/device-loss paths, not a correctness
   regression.

Impact: readback behavior on wgpu failure paths (device lost, long
delay, map_async disconnect) is silently unverified. A correctness
regression in \`PollType::Poll\` semantics between wgpu versions would
silently break screenshots and readback-based features.

Fix direction:
- Add a unit-level test with a mock/stub Device that controls
  \`poll()\` return timing and can force a Disconnected on the mpsc
  channel. Assert each of the four loop outcomes.
- Consider moving the readback loop into a small helper function
  that takes a \`Fn()\` poll closure + channel receiver, to make it
  unit-testable without a real GPU.
- If unit isolation is impractical, at minimum add an integration
  test that triggers a readback and asserts completion within a
  tight CPU budget (regression test for busy-wait blow-up).

Severity: medium — latent risk in a GPU-facing path that typically
works and has no automated coverage for failure modes.

 {"id":"ft-ibtjf","title":"[gh-52] build.rs must populate FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV for path-dep workspace layout","description":"## Problem (GitHub #52)

After the fail-closed guard from #44, `ft doctor` unconditionally reports `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded` on every clean-machine `cargo install --git ...` build. Root cause:

- `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs` only extracts vendored commit from `Cargo.lock` entries whose `source = "git+...#<hash>"` fragment carries a commit.
- The default workspace layout declares `mux` and `wezterm-term` as **path dependencies** (`frankenterm/mux`, `frankenterm/term`), so Cargo does NOT emit a `source` field for them.
- `select_vendored_metadata` returns `(None, None, None)`, no `cargo:rustc-env=FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV=...` is emitted, `option_env!("FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV")` resolves to `None`, and `vendored.rs` reports `Incompatible` at runtime.

Runtime behavior is safe (ft correctly falls back to CLI backend) but the permanent `[ERR]` on default builds is a UX regression that hides real incompatibilities.

## Fix direction

In `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs`, add a path-dep fallback branch to `select_vendored_metadata`:

1. If `mux.source.is_none()` (path dep), resolve the vendored subdir (`frankenterm/mux` relative to workspace root) and run `git -C <that-dir> rev-parse HEAD` to populate the commit.
2. Handle the case where the workspace is not a git checkout (e.g. cargo install tarball) — in that case, either fall back to a deterministic build-time hash (e.g. `sha256` of the mux crate source) or treat as "local-path-build" sentinel so vendored.rs can accept it without [ERR].
3. Add `cargo:rerun-if-changed=` entries for `frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml` and `frankenterm/term/Cargo.toml` so the metadata refreshes on vendored bumps.
4. Emit `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_VERSION` + `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_SOURCE` in the path-dep branch too.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git --bin ft frankenterm && ft doctor` no longer produces `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded`.
2. `ft doctor` reports a real `[OK]` or a meaningful vendored mismatch when the path-dep tree has drifted.
3. Runtime vendored compatibility guard (`vendored.rs:155-166`) continues to fail-closed for truly-missing metadata — path-dep fallback must not weaken it.
4. Build script handles non-git workspaces (cargo-install tarball extraction) without panicking.
5. Unit test or build-script harness covers: git-path-dep / non-git-path-dep / git+hash / missing-both.

## Reporter

GitHub user JordanChoo — https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm/issues/52","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:27:51.238506Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.805074Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.804717Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 3174399d (build.rs path-dep fallback + non-git sentinel + 8-test harness)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-icreu","title":"[MEDIUM] ActivityProfile::new allows NaN alpha/default_activity past clamp; poisons all hourly_ewma slots","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/survival.rs:437-438` — `ActivityProfile::new`.

ft-26k3h just hardened `update_hour` against NaN input. The construction-time path is the **complement**: `alpha.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` and `default_activity.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` both still let NaN through because `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN.

Two independent failure modes:

1. **NaN `default_activity`**: all 24 `hourly_ewma` slots are initialised to NaN. `predict_hour` returns NaN for every slot until the first clean `update_hour` overwrites the bucket via the `sample_count == 0` branch. Until then, every hazard/survival score computed from `predict` silently produces NaN (matches the ft-26k3h failure shape but from a different path).

2. **NaN `alpha`**: `self.alpha = NaN`. `update_hour` at line 460 computes `alpha.mul_add(sample, (1.0 - alpha) * prev) = NaN` on every non-first sample. `hourly_ewma[idx]` stays NaN from the second sample onward. ft-26k3h's entry guard doesn't catch this because the caller passes a clean `normalized_activity` — the NaN is stored in `self.alpha`, not in the input.

Both are the same `f64::clamp(NaN)` gotcha shipped across ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5 / ft-x50ce / ft-26k3h. This closes the pair's construction-time counterpart.

## Fix

Snap NaN to sensible defaults at the boundary:
- `alpha` → 0.1 (a conservative EWMA coefficient; config-adjacent code uses 0.3 as the common default but 0.1 is safe for an unspecified sentinel).
- `default_activity` → 0.5 (neutral midpoint — if operator didn't supply a meaningful default, 0.5 is the least-surprising fallback).

Both `.is_nan()` checks happen **before** the clamp so the clamp still trims out-of-range finite inputs normally.

## Tests

* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_alpha` — alpha=NaN → stored α=0.1, EWMA updates stay finite.
* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_default_activity` — default=NaN → all hourly_ewma slots = 0.5, predict_hour returns 0.5.

## Scope

Single file (`survival.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:55.543754Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995504Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ih4tm","title":"[MEDIUM] defer_fts_triggers=false is not reversible: triggers stay dropped after enabling once (ft-wk5fo follow-up)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`.

d74401c6 landed `StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers` (ft-wk5fo). The intent is a reversible opt-in: flip to `true` to trade FTS freshness for write-path latency, flip back to `false` to restore synchronous indexing. The inline comment at line 6006 says as much:

> The triggers can be re-created by re-opening with `defer_fts_triggers: false`

That claim is wrong. Concrete interleaving:

1. Operator opens DB with `defer_fts_triggers: true` on an up-to-date schema (`user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION`).
2. `initialize_schema` returns early at line 4315 (no SQL executed, up-to-date path).
3. The explicit `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS output_segments_ai/ad/au` at line 6010 runs — triggers gone.
4. Operator flips config to `defer_fts_triggers: false` and restarts.
5. `initialize_schema` again short-circuits at line 4315.
6. Line 6010 is skipped (flag is false). **No `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS` runs.**
7. Database silently stays in deferred mode; the operator's `false` setting is a no-op.

The inline comment at line 6007 hints the author knew: 'this commit ships the opt-in direction only; reverting would require re-running SCHEMA_SQL or the operator dropping the DB.' That matches reality but contradicts the line-6006 'can be re-created by re-opening' claim — and leaves the config behaving non-intuitively for operators.

## Severity rationale

- Not a crash or data loss.
- Silent wrong answer: operator flips back to `false`, expects synchronous FTS, gets deferred.
- One-way-door config is a known anti-pattern. Easy fix.

## Fix sketch

Make the open path truly bidirectional. On every open, after `initialize_schema` returns:

* If `defer_fts_triggers == true`: `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS ...` (idempotent).
* If `defer_fts_triggers == false`: `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS ...` using the same SQL the SCHEMA_SQL block defines.

Factor the trigger SQL into one private const referenced by both branches so SCHEMA_SQL and the re-create branch cannot drift.

## Tests

* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_roundtrip — open with true, verify triggers dropped, close, reopen with false, verify triggers re-created.
* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_idempotent_reopen — open with same flag twice back-to-back, no spurious SQL errors, trigger state stable.

## Scope

Single file (`storage.rs`). No API break (`defer_fts_triggers` already public, default `false`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:12.433144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095316Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095017Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-jqvg5","title":"Resolve frankenterm-core clippy -D warnings gate","description":"# Workspace-level clippy gate

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 356 verification sweep. The
`frankenterm-core` crate reports **17 clippy errors** when run with
`-D warnings`:

    cargo clippy --no-deps -p frankenterm-core \\
        --features distributed,asupersync-runtime,web \\
        --lib --tests -- -D warnings

All 17 errors are in files **unrelated to ft-xbnl0.2.4**:

- `src/ipc.rs`
- `src/robot_sdk_contracts.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs`
- `src/snapshot_engine.rs`
- `src/ui_query.rs`
- `src/workflows/engine.rs`
- `src/workflows/runner.rs`

These are from recent commits by other agents.

## Why not fix in ft-xbnl0.2.4
AGENTS.md explicitly instructs "NEVER ... disturb in ANY way the work of
other agents." The violations may be:
- awaiting `#[allow(...)]` annotations from their owners
- pending intentional restructures
- already planned for cleanup

Touching them without coordination could override the owners' planned
solutions.

## Fix categories observed (examples)
- `clippy::duration-suboptimal-units` — `Duration::from_millis(2_000)` should be `from_secs(2)`
- `clippy::redundant-closure` — `|| crate::foo()` should just be `crate::foo`
- `clippy::semicolon-if-nothing-returned` — trailing unit-valued expression without semicolon
- Several others (see `cargo clippy` output at time of fix)

## Suggested approach
For each file, coordinate with the owning agent (via Agent Mail or by checking
recent git history for active work) before applying fixes. Prefer:
1. Fixing real issues over `#[allow(...)]` annotations.
2. Single-file commits so each fix is easy to review.
3. Running `ubs` on each file after the clippy fix to avoid introducing
   new bugs in the cleanup.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The ft-xbnl0.2.4-scoped files (`src/distributed.rs`, `src/metrics.rs`,
`src/runtime_compat.rs`, `tests/web.rs`, `tests/ft_xbnl0_2_4_*.rs`) produce
zero clippy warnings.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.1`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FoggyRobin","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:55.194692Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170424Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","code-quality","workspace-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-jqvg5","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.540304Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-jz40p","title":"[MEDIUM] TimeSeries::percentile(NaN) returns smallest value instead of None","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/time_series.rs:239` — `TimeSeries::percentile_range` (and the `percentile` convenience that delegates to it).

Same class as ft-b4l62 (telemetry Histogram::quantile, fixed 2c7b891c). The function relies on `p.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` to validate its input, but `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN (documented Rust behaviour), so a NaN `p` cascades through:

```rust
let p = p.clamp(0.0, 1.0);                          // NaN stays NaN
let idx = ((values.len() - 1) as f64 * p)           // NaN
    .round() as usize;                              // NaN as usize = 0
Some(values[idx])                                   // smallest value
```

A NaN quantile arg silently returns the smallest sample — a plausible-looking number that has no relationship to the requested percentile. Pre-fix the Histogram analog had the same bug; the fix pattern is identical.

## Fix

```rust
if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() {
    return None;
}
```

Matches the ft-b4l62 disposition: NaN `p` is explicitly `None` rather than a silently-wrong small value.

## Tests

* `ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none` — pins None return for NaN p.
* `ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work` — regression: ±inf still clamp to 0.0/1.0 and return min/max samples (pre-existing contract, not broken by the new NaN guard).

## Scope

Single file (`time_series.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane1","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:11:47.750602Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756187Z","close_reason":"Fix and both regression tests already landed (time_series.rs:246-254 + :720-745). percentile_range now guards with 'if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() { return None; }' at line 252, so percentile(NaN) returns None instead of silently returning the smallest sample via NaN-clamp-to-0 → (NaN as usize)==0 cascade. Regression tests: ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none (NaN→None on both percentile + percentile_range, plus sanity check that valid p=0.5 still works) and ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work (±inf continue clamping to 0.0/1.0 → min/max sample, pre-existing contract preserved). Verified: 38 time_series:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-qhyfh","title":"[LOW][review] user_packs discovery skips sandbox_resolve_dir (ft-6mxfs follow-up)","description":"# Observation

From a review pass over 10 swarm commits in the last 2 hours (git log --since='2 hours ago'), specifically the ft-6mxfs canonicalization fix (0239fbd6).

## Context

ft-6mxfs closed a directory-boundary symlink escape in `.ft/patterns` discovery by routing through `sandbox_resolve_dir(&ws_dir, root_path)` before walking. The fix is correct and well-tested.

## The gap

patterns.rs:938 — when `config.user_packs_enabled = true`, the code takes a different branch:

```rust
if config.user_packs_enabled {
    if let Some(user_dir) = config.resolved_user_packs_dir() {
        let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&user_dir)?;
        ...
    }
}
```

This call does NOT route through `sandbox_resolve_dir`. The user-packs dir is either the user-configured `user_packs_dir` (tilde-expanded) or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/patterns`.

## Threat-model delta

Unlike `.ft/patterns` (inside a cloned repo — attacker-controlled), `~/.config/wa/patterns/` is under the user's own control. So the escape window is narrower — the user would have to set up the symlink themselves, at which point they already have write access.

However:
- If the user shares dotfiles across machines and a symlink in their config dir points at something sensitive on one machine but not another, a stray `wa` invocation could read files outside the intended scope.
- The inner per-file `sandbox_resolve` (ft-fbee5) still provides a per-YAML defense, so this is defense-in-depth, not a primary vulnerability.

## Proposed fix

Call `sandbox_resolve_dir(&user_dir, &user_dir.parent().unwrap_or(&user_dir))` or pass an explicit `user_sandbox_root` so the canonicalized-inside-root invariant extends to the user-packs path. Keep the behavior that a missing directory is Ok(None) (already the common case).

## Review context

Found via multi-pass review after shipping ft-h9g0q follow-up (retry.rs PaneNotFound classification). Rating LOW because it requires the user to be the attacker-of-themselves.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:39:18.533748Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:48:37.894024Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:48:37.893825Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qiba0","title":"[conformance] wa.mission_* / wa.tx_* (9 tools) lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

Nine transactional tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json` have manifest-freeze coverage (schema gate, additionalProperties=false) but no envelope roundtrip conformance test:

* `wa.mission_state` — MissionRunState enum serialization, agent/action assignment maps
* `wa.mission_abort` — abort outcome envelope, idempotency on already-aborted missions
* `wa.mission_explain` — explain payload shape, trace graph
* `wa.mission_pause` / `wa.mission_resume` — lifecycle state-machine transitions
* `wa.tx_plan` — MissionTxContract shape, precondition list, classification field
* `wa.tx_run` — phase state transitions, compensating action record
* `wa.tx_show` — include_contract flag behaviour, chain verification data
* `wa.tx_rollback` — rollback outcome record

## Fix shape

Follow the same pattern as `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` tests at mcp_conformance.rs:237-294 + the `agent_inventory_contract.json` golden shipped under ft-bs0ec (97ec6350). Recommended layout:

1. Fixture JSON per tool under `tests/fixtures/golden_robot_envelope/wa_{tool}.json` — canonical success envelope plus one denial/invalid-args envelope.
2. Test module `mcp_conformance_mission_tx.rs` that calls each tool with deterministic seeded inputs, canonicalises the response (BTreeMap ordering, strip volatile timestamps), and golden-compares.
3. Reuse helpers: `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, plus `UPDATE_GOLDEN=1` regen wiring from `agent_inventory_golden.rs`.

## Why this matters

Mission + Tx are the load-bearing transactional surface (precondition check, rollback, chain verification). A silent envelope drift here corrupts downstream client decision paths worse than a UI-level surface drift would — the transactional semantics are what callers depend on to unwind on failure. The mission_contract_golden.rs at line 80589845 pins MissionTxContract serde roundtrip but NOT the MCP envelope that wraps it.

## Scope guard

9 goldens + 9 tests + regen harness. ~500 LOC. No production changes unless drift is found during fixture capture — in which case a separate bug bead would be filed per drift.

## Explicitly out of scope

Core operator tools (wa.search/get_text/send/wait_for) — tracked under the separate core-tools conformance bead. Events/cass families (wa.events_*, wa.cass_*) — lower operator stakes; can wait for a later pass.","status":"blocked","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:50:18.619029Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:04:19.440844Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qispe","title":"hyperlink shape hash depends on HashMap insertion order","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:16:28.784823Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:21.337317Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:21.337038Z","close_reason":"Already landed in fe515b29: sort hyperlink params before compute_shape_hash so equal links hash identically regardless of HashMap insertion order; regression exercised locally with --features std.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-qyw1z","title":"[review][CRITICAL] mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs WIP uses std::env::set_var, violates forbid(unsafe_code) workspace lint","description":"Uncommitted WIP expansion of crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs (pane 2, ft-4o5mb success-path harness) calls std::env::set_var and std::env::remove_var 6 times across FakeWezterm::install and EnvGuard::Drop.

Rust 2024 edition made std::env::set_var/remove_var unsafe fn. The workspace declares [workspace.lints.rust] unsafe_code = "forbid" (Cargo.toml), so this test WILL NOT COMPILE once committed — the harness currently builds only because the uncommitted diff has not been fed through cargo check --all-targets.

Evidence:
- git diff crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs: 6 set_var/remove_var call sites in FakeWezterm::install() and impl Drop for EnvGuard.
- Cargo.toml workspace lints: unsafe_code = "forbid".
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/lib.rs:44: #![forbid(unsafe_code)] (reinforced at crate level).
- MEMORY.md already flags this constraint: 'std::env::set_var is unsafe in Rust 2024 edition — use Command::env() instead'.

Fix options (pick one, no new beads needed):
1. Drop the FakeWezterm env-var approach. Instead, pass FT_WEZTERM_CLI/FT_FAKE_WEZTERM_STATE_DIR via a builder API that threads explicit config into the MCP server (no process-global mutation).
2. Spawn the MCP server in a subprocess with Command::env("FT_WEZTERM_CLI", ...) and talk to it over stdio — Command::env is safe and the workspace policy already endorses this pattern.
3. Convert the integration test to a #[test] that uses a hand-rolled test harness struct carrying the CLI path + state dir through Config, not env vars.

 {"id":"ft-qzunb","title":"[HIGH] mux layout_arrangement + swap_layout JSON proptests fail on f64 ratio precision drift","description":"layout_arrangement_json_roundtrip and swap_layout_json_roundtrip in frankenterm/mux/tests/proptest_tab_wire_serde.rs currently fail at HEAD. arb_layout_arrangement generates LayoutArrangement::Split { ratio: 0.0f64..=1.0f64, ... } with recursive depth. When that ratio is serialized via serde_json and parsed back, some unrounded f64 values lose precision in the last decimal place (e.g. 0.10814827875804041 → 0.1081482787580404 and 0.9177432348398312 → 0.9177432348398313), so the roundtrip prop_assert_eq!(back, value) fails.

This is a blocker: the mux crate's cargo test currently shows 2/9 failing.

Fix direction: restrict the ratio generator to values that are exactly representable in IEEE 754 f64 with decimal notation (e.g. rounded to 1/2^16 fractions, or integer tenths/hundredths). Preferred: generate as integer 0..=10_000 and divide by 10_000.0 so every ratio is of the form n/10_000 and survives JSON's decimal representation. Keep the recursive depth/size parameters as-is.

Repro:
  CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ \
    CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-pane6-target \
    cargo test --manifest-path frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml --test proptest_tab_wire_serde

Observed at HEAD (04dc8d76).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T17:59:44.814922Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T18:01:15.542629Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T18:01:15.542244Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 10309c8e: constrain arb_layout_arrangement's ratio to (0..=10_000).map(|n| n as f64 / 10_000) so every generated ratio is of the form n/10_000 and survives a serde_json decimal roundtrip bit-for-bit. All 10 tests in frankenterm/mux/tests/proptest_tab_wire_serde.rs now pass (was 7 pass / 2 fail). Granularity 0.0001 is more than adequate for split layout wire data.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["codec","mux","proptest","regression","serde"]}

## Findings table

| # | Location | Category | Verdict |
|---|----------|----------|---------|
| 1-29 | mux/src/pane.rs:603-775 + mux/src/tab.rs:4027-5100+ | 29x unimplemented!() in FakePane test doubles | False positive - inside #[cfg(test)] modules (pane.rs:595, tab.rs:4004). Legitimate Rust pattern: test doubles only impl trait methods the tests exercise. |
| 30 | term/src/terminalstate/mod.rs:141 | // TODO: selective_erase when supported (struct-field comment) | Documentation of known terminal-emulator limitation. Not a stub - the struct works without this field. |
| 31 | term/src/terminalstate/mod.rs:1251 | // TODO: see https://vt100.net/docs/vt510-rm/DECSTR.html in Device::SoftReset | Reference comment. The SoftReset handler is fully implemented below - comment is just a spec pointer. |
| 32-33 | term/src/test/c0.rs:13,36 | // TODO: when we can set the left margin, we should test that here | Test-coverage gap. Tests still run; two cases arent covered because the test framework doesnt expose left-margin setup. |
| 34 | term/src/test/mod.rs:9 | // mod selection; FIXME: port to render layer | Historical module disable. The selection test module was moved to the render layer; this comment is a bread-crumb. |
| 35 | mux/src/ssh.rs:957 | fn process_id(&self) -> Option<u32> { None } | Correct by design - WrappedSshChild has no local PID because the child process runs on the remote SSH host. None is the honest answer. |
| 36 | mux/src/ssh.rs:962 | fn as_raw_handle(&self) -> Option<RawHandle> { None } (Windows) | Same rationale as #35. |

## Corrects prior record

The closed bead ft-yv02l (2026-04-20 'Audit placeholder/stub markers surfaced by mock-code-finder sweep') listed the mux tab.rs and pane.rs unimplemented!() clusters under 'Cat 2' and deferred them as 'out-of-scope, pane 1 (mux) territory' requiring domain-owner review. That classification was incorrect - these are test scaffolding inside #[cfg(test)] modules. There is no pane-1-owner action required.

- 0 production stubs

- 0 commits required
